q: Everything around us is made up of elements, molecules, or ___.
The attractive forces that hold atoms together in compounds are also known as ___.
The outermost shell electrons (___) of those atoms are the basis for understanding the joining of atoms.

In order to explain the electronic structures of atoms, the principles of filling electrons in the atomic orbitals, such as the ___, Pauli’s exclusion principle, and Hund’s rule are fundamental to understand.
Each subshell can be filled with 2, 6, 10, and 14 electrons, ___.
$s$ subshell has only one ___ orbital.
There are three dumbbell-shaped $p$ orbitals and the five $d$ orbitals have ___ shapes.
According to the Aufbau principle (Aufbau means ___ in German), electrons are filled in the lower energy atomic orbitals before filling higher energy ones.
One of the properties of the electron, known as ___, has to be considered.
Each electron spins on an axis, like a ___.
Pauli’s exclusion principle states that no more than two electrons can occupy the same orbital, and two electrons in the same orbital must have ___ spins.
Hund’s rule states that every orbital in a subshell is ___ occupied with one electron before any one orbital is doubly occupied with opposite spins.
The electrons in the valence shells (outermost shells) of an atom are known as the ___.
For an atom, the number of valence electrons is equal to its ___ in the Periodic Table.
Whenever a chemical bond forms, atoms lose, gain, or share electrons in order to achieve the stable electronic structures of ___.
Eight electrons in the valence shell is known as the ___.
The chemically important valence electrons occupied in $s$ and $p$ orbitals are represented using ___ introduced by Gilbert Newton Lewis.
The strength of sharing or transferring the valence electrons depends on the ___ of the atoms involved in the chemical bonding.
Since electronegativity (EN) is a measure of the ability of an atom to attract the ___ to itself, the greater an element’s electronegativity, the stronger its attractive power to electrons will be.
___ is the amount of energy released when an electron is added to a gaseous atom.
___ is the amount of energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.
The elements having high electron affinity easily gain electrons, resulting in the formation of the ___.
The elements having low ionisation energy easily lose electrons, resulting in the formation of the ___.
The type of chemical bonding depends on the types of elements (___) that are involved.
